overview: This volume focuses on the Dalalah (semantic and contextual implication) of the Hadith of Ghadir. The author systematically refutes Fakhr al-Razi and Shah Abdul Aziz Dehlavi's linguistic argument that 'Mawla' cannot mean 'Awla' (more rightful/superior). He then proves the contextual link of Ghadir to Imamate by demonstrating through numerous Sunni sources that the Verse of Tabligh (5:67), the Verse of Ikmal (5:3), and the Verse of Sa'ala Sa'il (70:1) were all revealed regarding Ali at Ghadir Khumm. Finally, he cites the poetry of Hassan b. Thabit and Qays b. Sa'd to show that the early companions explicitly understood the Prophet's declaration of 'Mawla' to mean 'Imam' and 'Hadi'.

responses_to_dehlavi.dehlavi_claim_addressed: Hadith of Ghadir Khumm: Shia say: The word 'Mawla' means 'Awla bi al-Tasarruf' (the one with the most right to rule/administer). Therefore, Ali is the designated Caliph. Dehlavi's attack: He accepts the hadith as authentic but completely rejects the Shia linguistic and contextual interpretation.
responses_to_dehlavi.author_response_summary: The author refutes the linguistic objection (originating from Fakhr al-Razi) that synonyms must be perfectly interchangeable in all grammatical constructs (e.g., Razi argued you cannot say 'Huwa Mawla min Fulan' instead of 'Awla min Fulan'). The author cites Sunni linguists and Usul scholars to prove that synonyms do not require absolute interchangeability due to specific Arabic usage rules. He lists numerous Sunni exegetes and linguists (Abu Ubaydah, Al-Farra, Al-Zajjaj, Al-Akhfash) who explicitly interpreted 'Mawla' as 'Awla'.
key_evidence_cited: Statements of Abu Ubaydah, Al-Farra, Al-Zajjaj, Al-Akhfash

responses_to_dehlavi.dehlavi_claim_addressed: Hadith of Ghadir Khumm: He accepts the hadith as authentic but completely rejects the Shia linguistic and contextual interpretation.
responses_to_dehlavi.author_response_summary: The author proves the contextual connection of Ghadir to Imamate by citing Sunni consensus and narrations that the Verse of Tabligh (5:67) was revealed commanding the Prophet to declare Ali's Wilayah, and the Verse of Ikmal (5:3) was revealed immediately after the declaration, signifying the completion of the religion through Imamate.
key_evidence_cited: Narrations from Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir

responses_to_dehlavi.dehlavi_claim_addressed: Hadith of Ghadir Khumm: He accepts the hadith as authentic but completely rejects the Shia linguistic and contextual interpretation.
responses_to_dehlavi.author_response_summary: The author cites Sunni sources proving that the Verse of 'Sa'ala Sa'il' (70:1) was revealed when Al-Harith b. al-Nu'man al-Fihri objected to the Prophet's declaration of Ali's Wilayah at Ghadir and asked God to send a stone upon him if it was truly from God.

responses_to_dehlavi.dehlavi_claim_addressed: Hadith of Ghadir Khumm: He accepts the hadith as authentic but completely rejects the Shia linguistic and contextual interpretation.
responses_to_dehlavi.author_response_summary: The author cites the poetry of Hassan b. Thabit recited at Ghadir with the Prophet's approval, and the poetry of Qays b. Sa'd at Siffin, both explicitly using the words 'Imam' and 'Hadi' as the direct meaning of the Prophet's declaration 'Mawla'. This proves the companions understood it as political and spiritual leadership.

biographical_dossiers.evaluation_summary: Highly praised as a leading Imam, Hafiz, and critic of Hadith. His Tafsir is considered one of the most authentic, as he committed to only including sound reports.
Acknowledged as a great Hafiz, historian, and reliable authority in Rijal and Hadith.
Described as a famous Hafiz, one of the greatest scholars of Hadith, and author of Hilyat al-Awliya.
Recognized as the Imam of his time in Tafsir, grammar, and linguistics, and author of the famous Asbab al-Nuzul.
Praised as the unique scholar of his time in Tafsir, highly reliable in transmission, and praised by Al-Qushayri.
Revered as the Hafiz of the Levant, a massive authority in history and Hadith, and author of Tarikh Dimashq.
Highly respected Hanafi Chief Justice, historian, and author of Umdat al-Qari (commentary on Sahih al-Bukhari).
Respected as the Mufti and historian of Medina, a great Shafi'i scholar, and author of Wafa' al-Wafa and Jawahir al-Iqdayn.
Defended against Ibn Kathir's weakening; shown to be authenticated by Ahmad b. Hanbal, Yahya b. Ma'in, Al-Bukhari, and Al-Tirmidhi.

argument_lineage_traced.claim: The word 'Mawla' cannot mean 'Awla' because synonyms must be perfectly interchangeable in all grammatical constructs (e.g., you cannot say 'Huwa Mawla min Fulan').
chain: Fakhr al-Razi (d. 606) -> Al-Iji (d. 756) -> Al-Jurjani (d. 816) -> Shah Abdul Aziz Dehlavi (d. 1239)

rebuttal_lineage_cited.what_they_refuted: Refuted Al-Iji and Al-Jurjani's linguistic argument that 'Mawla' cannot mean 'Awla', demonstrating that Arabic usage does not require absolute interchangeability of synonyms.
